one_line_takeaway: The first defense benchmark for IQA metrics shows that cheap compression-based purification (DiffJPEG/JPEG) is the best practical defender, certified methods are strongest but too slow, and classification-favorite diffusion purification (DiffPure) transfers poorly to IQA -- with randomness essential against adaptive attacks and objective quality metrics prone to misranking defenses versus human judgment.
key_finding: Compression-based purification is the most effective purification family because adversarial perturbations are largely unnatural high-frequency noise that JPEG/DiffJPEG remove while preserving genuine structure: DiffJPEG leads purifications on SROCCadv, Dscore and Rscore. Certified methods give the best overall robustness/correlation combination but are impractically slow (fastest certified method is ~4x slower than the slowest purification). Adversarial training yields the best correlations but worse Dscore and does not purify the image. Adaptive attacks are far more damaging than non-adaptive ones, and randomness plus geometric transformation (e.g., Random Rotate, Crop, DiffPure) is key to surviving adaptive attacks (Flip fails precisely because it lacks randomness). Diffusion purification (DiffPure), state-of-the-art for classification, underperforms for IQA -- it introduces its own artifacts giving the worst correlations and lower image quality -- underscoring that classification defenses do not transfer to IQA and need task-specific adaptation. Transformer-based metrics (MANIQA, CLIP-IQA+, TOPIQ) are intrinsically more robust (higher no-defense Rscore) via global self-attention; custom FPR is the most vulnerable. Subjective study reverses some objective rankings: Real-ESRGAN gives the best perceived quality (even beating the no-defense case) while Gaussian blur and DiffJPEG look worse subjectively than their PSNR/SSIM suggest -- objective similarity metrics can mislead leaderboards.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating DEFENSES against adversarial attacks on neural-network-based no-reference (NR) Image Quality Assessment (IQA) metrics. Prior work (notably the authors' own Antsiferova et al. 2024) benchmarked the vulnerability of IQA metrics but did not study defenses; this paper fills that gap. Its own contributions are: (1) a unified methodology and evaluation scheme systematizing 30 defense algorithms of three types (17 purification, 7 adversarial-training, 6 certified) against 14 adversarial attacks (white- and black-box) in both non-adaptive and adaptive settings across 9 NR IQA metrics and 4 datasets; (2) new evaluation metrics tailored to IQA defense (Dscore and its purified variant D(D)score measuring how well a defense restores original IQA scores, plus adaptations of Rscore, and certified radius / certified relative delta for certified methods); (3) a large-scale crowd-sourced subjective study (2,700+ participants, 60,000+ responses) to assess perceptual quality of defended images and to label a new released adversarial-image dataset usable for adversarial training and attack detection; and (4) an open online leaderboard. The task differs from classification defense because a good IQA defense must simultaneously restore the original metric score, preserve correlation with subjective quality (SROCC/PLCC with MOS), AND preserve perceptual image quality.
threat_model: Adversary perturbs an input image within an epsilon-bounded budget (tested at 2/4/8 out of 255 with SSIM/LPIPS constraints, and weak/medium/strong presets) to maximize (inflate) an NR IQA metric's predicted quality score, formalized as A(x)=argmax over x' with rho(x',x)<=eps of L(f_w(x')). Motivation is real-world manipulation: gaming search-engine image rankings (e.g., Bing uses IQA to rank), inflating public benchmark/competition results for image/video processing and compression algorithms, and corrupting content-quality assessment. Attacks come in white-box (gradient access, e.g., APGD, I-FGSM, IQA-specific attacks) and black-box (e.g., Parsimonious, Square-attack family) variants. Two attacker knowledge settings are distinguished: non-adaptive (attacker targets only the IQA model, unaware of the defense) and adaptive (defense is made differentiable and folded into the attacked pipeline so the attacker leverages gradients of both metric and defense). Defense goal: preserve validity of the IQA score under attack while keeping perceptual quality.
limitations: Adversarial training was evaluated on only 2 of the 9 IQA metrics (Linearity, Koncept) and only in the adaptive setting; certified methods were evaluated only in the non-adaptive setting and on a very small 10-image KonIQ subset (Table 3), so AT and certified results rest on limited coverage. Heavy subsampling for tractability (1,000-image subsets of KonIQ/KADID; only 50 images per dataset for black-box attacks and certified defenses) despite reported statistical-validity tests. Strong defenses restore scores but sharply reduce correlation with subjective quality, so the strongest defenses are impractical; most defenses collapse under strong adaptive attacks (only DiffPure and DiffJPEG remain notable exceptions). PSNR/SSIM are meaningless for geometric transforms (Flip, Rotate, Crop) and objective quality metrics generally misrepresent perceived quality. LPIPS could not be used to measure defended-image quality due to attack transferability. Applying classification-based randomized smoothing to a regression task via discretization is theoretically questionable (the authors note it but rely on it). Certified relative delta is infinite for classification-based methods, limiting cross-method comparability. Focus is restricted to NR (no-reference) metrics and to score-inflation attacks.

datasets_used: KonIQ-10k (10,073 natural images; 1,000-image subset used for most experiments, 50 for black-box/certified)
KADID-10k (10,125 artificially distorted images; 1,000-image sampled subset)
NIPS 2017 Adversarial Learning Development Set (1,000 images)
New released adversarial-image dataset with 60,000+ subjective (MOS) responses from 2,700+ participants
quantitative_results: Scope: 30 defenses (17 purification + 7 adversarial training + 6 certified), 14 attacks (white+black box, 3 strength levels), 9 NR IQA metrics, 4 datasets; ~25,000 GPU-hours on A100 80GB
Purification, no defense baseline (Table 2, non-adaptive/adaptive): SROCCadv 0.413/0.413, Dscore(D) 66.68/66.68, Rscore 0.56/0.56
DiffJPEG (best purification, Table 2): SROCCadv 0.608/0.549, Dscore(D) 12.94/29.81, Rscore 1.07/0.71, SROCCclear 0.625/0.610, 8.11 ms
JPEG purification: SROCCadv 0.605, Dscore(D) 13.07, Rscore 1.07 (non-adaptive), 227.34 ms
DiffPure: worst correlations among strong methods, SROCCclear 0.496/0.487, SROCCadv 0.485/0.470, but low Dscore(D) 2.01/22.96; slowest purification at 691.42 ms
DISCO: Dscore(D) 3.51 non-adaptive (top-3 Rscore 1.14) but drops under adaptive attack (Dscore(D) 47.91), 139.60 ms
Adversarial training (Table 3, adaptive, Koncept+Linearity): APGD-SSIM-2 best with SROCCclear 0.830, SROCCadv 0.763, D(D)score 17.11, Rscore 1.23; NT (Liu 2024) SROCCadv 0.649, Rscore 0.81

Certified (Table 3, non-adaptive, 10 KonIQ images): DensePure DP(C) SROCCadv 0.815, D(D)score 1.09, Rscore 6.20, 82,130 ms; DDRS(C) SROCCadv 0.792, Rscore 6.21, 39,800 ms; RS(C) highest certified radius 0.183 (Rscore 5.61); Denoised Median Smoothing DMS(R) lowest certified relative delta 1.440, SROCCadv 0.822
Subjective study: 2,700+ participants, 60,000+ responses; Real-ESRGAN rated best perceptual quality, surpassing even the no-defense case
Fastest certified method (~2,830-11,080 ms) is roughly 4x slower than the slowest purification method
baselines_compared: Purification: Gaussian blur, Median blur, JPEG, Color Quantization, DiffJPEG, Unsharp, Flip, Bilinear Upscale, Resize, Random Rotate, Random Crop, Random Noise, FCN, MPRNet, Real-ESRGAN, DISCO, DiffPure
Adversarial training: Classic AT (Chistyakova et al. 2024, APGD with LPIPS/SSIM MOS-adjustment at budgets 2/4/8), Gradient-Norm/NT (Liu et al. 2024)
Certified: Randomized Smoothing (Cohen 2019), Denoised RS (Salman 2020), Diffusion DRS (Carlini 2022), DensePure (Chen 2022b), Median Smoothing (Chiang 2020), Denoised Median Smoothing
Target IQA metrics: Meta-IQA, MANIQA, CLIP-IQA+, TOPIQ, Koncept, SPAQ, PAQ2PIQ, Linearity, FPR
